imagination but i enjoyed the project i was working on and decided to continue with it into my phd but after a couple years into it i decided not to stay in academia it was really a combination of things the many failed projects seeing the horrible long work hours to compensation ratio that is post doc life i m not opposed to long hours but i would expect to be reasonably compensated for them and the horrible career opportunities generally in academia after seeing the dozen stellar candidates that came to campus for each new tenure track position to give job talks i realized that i would never be competitive for those positions as an alternative i ve been set on finding work somewhere in government or industry i felt that my skills were well suited to data science and it s a field that is in hot demand right now and has good career paths and often wants phd qualifications well i don t want to give away anything too personal my work involves a lot of computer based analysis of data broadly speaking the research i do is in the area of psychology mainly my work involves coding both experiments and programs to analyze biological signals in matlab and the usual statistics and visualizations to go along with it two years ago i realized i needed to do more extra curricular projects to make me a more competitive candidate for data science positions i knew i needed to at least showcase my programming skills in an environment outside of matlab i did two side projects in python one doing some sports analytics and the other a jupyter notebook to analyze data from a survey looked at non data scientist jobs for phds a lot of those are industry research positions that want someone with a very specific research background often in molecular biology pharmacology or a not quite a good enough a programmer and lack the formal computer science background to apply for programmer positions has anyone else here been in a similar situation and have advice on finding work Name: 50_ml_ai ml_phd_ai
Representative_Docs: ['i can tell you as a math major who have been in the workforce a few years that most jobs that don t require a phd simply aren t that mathematical i really wish this was not the case but it is even the jobs that are considered mathematical are mostly about trying to code the math into the computer and you have libraries that does a lot of heavy lifting for you the math you use most likely isn t that advanced like matrix multiplication or linear regression ols math is all done on computers now and i would argue that this actually makes it quite important to know things like cloud computing servers etc because this is where your computer will do the math nobody is doing it by hand outside of the ivory towers you will also never have to prove anything those are for the phds literally no company is hiring fresh out of school newcomers to do pde or topological data analysis which is an actual thing data science and machine learning is like cleaning manipulating wrangling data i ve learned with a lot of disappointment that even with master s you are competing with phds for many of these advanced math jobs which automatically puts you at a handicap without the doctoral degree however i have heard that quant finance is more open to non phd people now although you still have a lot of quant jobs that are specifically geared towards math physics phds i ve also heard that supply chain and operations is increasingly computerized and algorithm driven with all the data now', 'i keep seeing how ai ml jobs require phds or a master as a minimum should one even software engineering fields just like information security you need a lot of education just to get an entry level position while some programmers start working right away and make far more money', 'i am a phd student in south asian country my advisor yesterday was currently ranting how most of the faculty in the department have a research interest in machine learning with a primary focus on an allied discipline and not computer science and for that there are far less faculty who can effectively teach and research in core computer science i knew about this situation in my department but i didnt expected to see this even in top colleges in states also my research interest is architecture and ml yet i failed to find any faculty in my research filed at top colleges except who were quite old and had the same research interest as me most of the people in cs department was largely filled with people with their interest in ml and in more than few cases had no background in core cs at all this is not the same in architecture even in other subjects like compiler operating system i struggle to find any young people in the cs department even in the top colleges that makes me thing why just dont make an another ai department in the top colleges i know ai and ml are in demand so wont it make more sense to just create an another department for that although many have done it also makes me feel sad that with advent of ml ai the core computer science research area is struggling']

Name: 53_cs_ml_ds_ai ml
Representative_Docs: ['i m hoping to get a career in ml ai after school and am currently an undergrad student at the university of utah as of right now i am a data science major but i just discovered that the cs degree offers an artificial intelligence emphasis it may seem like an obvious choice to take the ai emphasis but the electives i would be opting to take to get the ai cs degree are actually already requirements to get the ds degree the extra classes i would be taking by getting a ds degree instead of a cs degree are also not only a lot more interesting to me but it seems a lot more relevant to ai ml and other subfields i m interested in bioinformatics ds has classes like machine learning applied ml in bmi scientific data visualization and stochastic processes cs has classes like computer systems parallel programming and computer graphics the issue is that while i feel that ds is a more relevant degree and has the same classes and then some as the ai track in the cs major the name of the degree i have will probably matter in the job search process does it actually matter should i take the major that i feel will prepare me less because it might give me more job opportunities no matter which degree i get i m planning on getting a masters in cs with an emphasis on data analysis which will include much more in depth courses like clustering deep learning probabilistic learning etc so does this even matter cheers', 'if i remember correctly the uk and ireland have something called conversion master s degrees in computer science which are designed for people with undergraduate degrees from pretty much any field i m not sure whether those are a thing in the us though i haven come across them so far i also didn t actively look so that may be a lack of information on my part generally i d imagine that it would be very hard to make a case for being accepted to a phd program in computer science with your background the reason for that isn t necessarily that your background isn t in computer science as quite a few people enter graduate programs from entirely different fields but that you may have neither the necessary basic mathematics nor the at least introductory programming background if possible you could provide a bit more information about that your best bet may be getting accepted to a master s degree program explicitly in computer science not information technology or similar related fields if done at a reasonably reputable university you could then use that degree as your potential entry ticket to graduate programs if i had to make an analogy i am more of an elon musk kind of guy rather than a social scientist while i don t think it makes sense to compare oneself to very select success stories he doesn t have a phd he also had the benefit of making a very large sum of money off his initial companies to fund or enter the ones he s currently known for currently my interest lie in artificial intelligence on a related note you should first check whether computer science would actually be the right choice for you with no experience in what to expect from that field terms like ai certainly sound interesting but many people are surprised that large chunks of the latter are simply mathematics and statistics instead of some arcane ai magic especially when it comes to machine learning if you are mostly looking for machine learning as that s the sub field that made headlines over the past decade the recordings of andrew ng s stanford course are a great introduction and should give you a sense of what that subject entails', 'i briefly looked through your history and noticed you have more years in undergrad if you re scared that you don t have a solid foundation then start building one you can consider machine learning as spicy statistics applied mathematics first make sure you address the pros and cons of pursuing a graduate degree and if you re actually interested in the subject matter you may or may not be that person and i could be flat out wrong but make sure you don t idealize a higher degree or a specific subject matter until you actually try it and take into account your finances and time costs some options apply for a minor in cs math stats take courses that you think will be foundational apply for a dual in above this one is likely less feasible if you want to graduate on time and could also depend on your work ethic and how your school sets curricula take foundational courses as electives in the topics you re interested in that are once again foundational self study pick one of all will lead you to your goal unless you re absolutely sure you want to get into machine learning and artificial intelligence i d pick make sure you plan out your credits graduating on time or early and fit in interesting classes around that you should also talk to your advisor about which courses to take to help you transition to help you determine what courses may be foundational depending on your school you should be able to add a minor after you take courses so is pretty much the same as the minor is really more for name if you re ee i m assuming you ve likely taken a multivariable calculus course and you ve had an introduction to probability if you re absolutely sure of pursuing a graduate degree the courses out of the scope of your major that i d recommend looking into are an introductory proof based course linear algebra which is likely going to be a lot of theory and proofs any sort of introduction to ml ai courses which tend to offer a more shallow overview of the material and an introductory optimization course if you can fit in more advanced course s senior year that d be ideal i think by the time you get there though you ll have a better feel of what you want to do i m not very familiar with m s programs directly with machine learning but m s cs degrees tend to be flexible along with applied math and stats depending on the schools you apply to generally i d say an m s in cs is a safe bet as for me i m doing an m s under applied mathematics and i d say about of my courses revolve around ml and the other are analysis optimization courses which in a sense also are somewhat ml oriented i think in your scenario you re getting a bit ahead of yourself imo you should first address if you re seriously interested in the subject matter i m sure there s a little overlap but are you sure that you re interested in ml even though you ve been taking courses in ee even before that if you haven t taken the foundational courses for a field how can you be sure of an m s or even a phd in the field and then address if you need a higher degree for your career goals']

Name: 55_web_job_development_software
Representative_Docs: ['i m a june grad from a top liberal arts college where i majored in an interdisciplinary cs design humanities degree i had been planning on going on to a phd program in cs hci until early in my senior year when i decided to take a year in industry instead because i was getting seriously burnt out i m now in a nontechnical role at a mature startup i ve done very well in this role and found i really enjoy the fast pace and opportunities for advancement i m leaning away from the idea of going back to school in the near future and thinking about moving into a software engineering role i think it would be more intellectually stimulating than what i m doing now i love cs and i want to have a deep understanding of how tech products are built in the long term i d like to work in a leadership role at the intersection of business strategy and engineering so now i m formulating a plan for how i can make this switch i m thinking i d like to actually change jobs years from now my goal is to get an entry level software engineering role with the following qualities the organization values engineering best practices and takes relatively good care of its codebase the engineering culture values doing things well and there is a standard for readability and maintainability of new code the engineering org has a healthy collaborative culture where questions and Name: 60_bootcamp_gt_boot_leetcode
Representative_Docs: ['i recently graduated from the university of minnesota trilogy data analytics amp visualization boot camp i highly recommend the program but only if you are serious about wanting to learn it s a time commitment outside of class a lot of studying tinkering reviewing solutions on stack overflow and w schools com etc did i learn a lot the first day of class i knew very little about coding didn t even know what bash was i had acted as a product manger for some software development projects did my fair share of software testing looking for bugs but didn t know how to write any code so i was familiar with some of the lingo and processes but didn t know how to write a single line of code my final project was machine learning classifying sec k risk factor text documents for a professor at the u a side project i did was scrapping tweets and performing a sentiment analysis for a unc professor i learned a ton be prepared for class class is fast paced classes had two components instructor led examples and student in class activities that was a combined in class activities in months the activities are where i learned a lot it s practicing what you were just taught and then getting immediate feedback on the right way to complete the activity a lot of my learning was figuring out why my code wasn t working then making sure i didn t continue to make the same syntax mistakes we had homework assignments and projects they give you the option to complete the easy intermediate or hard assignments i always did the hard assignments every one of them was challenging and took hours to complete positives instructors there were two cohorts that met on separate nights during the week and together on the weekends the instructors would alternate for the combined saturday class both were data scientists by day and very knowledgeable both on their own time would post articles job openings and other resources to the cohort slack channel instructors are probably a big reason why trilogy has some good and bad reviews i got lucky having two great instructors that took an interest in making sure we were learning we also had great tas maybe our classes just got lucky not a single person dropped out of the class between the two classes people made it the entire months and graduated surveys made a difference every week you are required to fill out a class survey to access your student portal for the first months every week i responded please give us a syllabus trilogy never gave us a syllabus but our instructor finally started slacking out what we were going to be covering in the upcoming week very helpful i saw this week on our class page that trilogy posted a syllabus for future cohorts what s my point i felt that they listened to student feedback and made incremental changes throughout the months this might not be the case at every university but the umn trilogy success manager and the instructors seemed to take the feedback and tried to implement improvements along the way class videos every class session is recorded so you can watch it later the first few months i didn t use that often but when the content started getting more difficult and i referred to the videos quite a bit negatives they didn t provide a syllabus this was frustrating because i wanted to spend time preparing at home so when i heard about a new function in class it wouldn t be the first time career services isn t what i thought it was when i signed up but it has been satisfactory after i adjusted my expectations career services is meant to get you interview ready not a placement service that being said a few weeks before graduation they announced that we would be having a demo days optional for students that would be interested in participating there are quite a few decent companies pre registered to attend us bank united health care wells fargo thomson reuters best buy etc the admissions process was a little confusing and misleading many of the students didn t realize until the st day of class that this was a trilogy education program not umn the admissions people used umn edu emails but once class started the support emails were all trilogy we took classes at the u but we weren t umn students that being said my certificate says university of minnesota in big font in partnership with trilogy education services in smaller font and is signed by umn deans misleading at the beginning but i got the umn certificate at the end what was most surprising how much we learned in such a short period of time i wouldn t consider myself an expert or fluent in the languages libraries we were exposed to but i did learn how to find answers examples library supplements etc for example now i feel very comfortable importing connecting to a Name: 61_review_literature_tools_literature review
Representative_Docs: ['hi everyone i m planning to conduct a systematic review as part of my academic research my focus is on exploring a timely and recent topic the addictive potential of short form videos like those on tiktok instagram reels etc i d love to hear your recommendations for ai powered tools that can help streamline the following tasks advanced literature search identifying relevant articles and studies in academic databases data extraction summarizing key information from multiple documents visualization creating graphs concept maps or citation network diagrams any other tools that might be helpful for this type of project additionally i d appreciate any general advice on integrating ai into the research process or hearing about personal experiences from others who ve used ai for systematic reviews thanks in advance for your help', 'been trying out tools to make the literature review process more efficient i m interested in knowing how you guys have been using ai for that part i don t have many tools in my stack and i prefer to use free ones if possible but there are a few i ve been using for the past few months right now i use preliminary search finding research papers abstract summary extract bibliographic information nelima keyword search to get knowledgeable on the topic summary of other related research papers this is a life saver adhd was going to be the death of me comparing and contrasting methodologies sources results nelima search engine for other online articles perplexity grammar and writing help if needed gpt note taking my thoughts if it s related to my research and if i feel like it otter ai i know most people probably don t use many tools for the literature review but if you do are there any other tools you would recommend', 'fellow grad students if you re knee deep in your lit review and feeling like you re drowning in a sea of pdfs you re not alone as a phd candidate in psychology my dissertation lit review required synthesizing sources on cognitive behavioral therapy applications manually extracting themes noting biases and ensuring comprehensive coverage it was a recipe for burnout that s why i ve leaned into ai tools designed for academic texts and they ve cut my research time in half without sacrificing quality first off the key is choosing tools that go beyond basic summarization a good literature review generator doesn t just spit out bullet points it organizes sources thematically and suggests interconnections for example i inputted my core keywords into textero s literature review tool uploaded a few key pdfs and it generated a structured overview main arguments counterpoints and even methodological critiques it flagged how older studies on cbt evolved with modern neuro imaging data which became a pivotal section in my review this isn t just convenient it helps spot research gaps early like underexplored cultural adaptations in therapy pair that with a reference finder feature and you re golden for expanding your bibliography instead of endless database dives it scans for related citations and verifies their relevance in my workflow i d generate the initial review then use the finder to add more sources per theme the free tier handles smaller batches which is perfect for iterating during early drafts of course accuracy is paramount in academia i always cross check ai suggestions against originals tools can hallucinate but ones with built in citation verification minimize that an ai fixer came in handy too for paraphrasing dense excerpts without losing meaning it made my writing more concise while keeping the scholarly tone pros faster synthesis means more time for original analysis cons over reliance might dull critical thinking so i limit it to of the process for freelancers or lecturers prepping courses these could streamline prep work similarly how do you handle lit reviews has anyone tried ai for interdisciplinary topics drop tips or tools you ve found reliable let s build a resource list']

Name: 62_degree_humanities_stem_people
Representative_Docs: ['i m a professor passing classes and ticking the boxes to get the degree is not enough for a good job in most fields or likely helping you have the social life you will want long term seniors come and talk to me about this regularly i ll take this in three parts classes academics ecs social life classes first passing classes doesn t mean much these days especially if you are using ai people might not like it but that s the truth most classes are no longer solely based on a high stakes exam or term paper and instead give more credit for formative assignments like homework this is aimed at encouraging students to do the work and reduce stress levels but means somebody who passes with a d and gets their degree likely doesn t know a whole lot about their field compared with the students who got higher grades jobs aren t about meeting a set standard it s a rack and stack competition knowing something helps you in that competition norms and relative performance is relevant here sometimes students come to me and say nobody knows this or nobody passed this exam or nobody really comes to class every day or nobody actually does the reading false false and false my and many of my colleagues have very realistic exam homework problems and an appropriate number of students get as and even perfect scores you are competing for jobs against students who do the reading go to class and office hours etc ecs extracurriculars and internships especially help you in that job hunting competition they give you context resume bullets and things to talk about in interviews again it s not did you tick the boxes it s are you the best candidate in this competition for most fields having a good answer to the question tell me about a project you managed or how do you manage disagreements on a team or whatever they ask in interviews is easier when you have done more than classes and video games there are exceptions where that is less true like nursing but that field has more structured clinical built in internship and many nurses get hired based on connections made in clinicals and a non trivial licensing exam nclex which goes to point one above knowing your stuff social life social life is more than parties it s building a network of mutually supportive people you are unlikely to have such a rich opportunity for this again unless you go into a high stress all consuming career like medicine or the military from what i ve seen where work is your life so your work people become your family and maybe you also all get shot at or covered in blood together there s a reason so many people are close to their college friends for life why even when you lose touch a bit years after you graduate and maybe haven t spoken in years they call you and say hey i m getting married and i know it s a flight for you but i d love if you could come it takes work but it pays dividends to everyone involved it s people you meet up for coffee between classes or go to the gym or give a ride to when their car gets towed study together and on and on i talk to a lot of seniors who have been just existing sometimes they complain that we the college haven t done enough to e g help them get a job and i ll be honest it is frustrating because it is always the students who have never been to the career center never attended the career fairs on campus x per year not been a member of our department s three clubs which combined bring in employers per semester not been to our department s mini career fairs x per year big employers in attendance usually hiring for multiple roles and internships not done our department s internship course option credit for internships etc the conversation goes like this nobody told me there are posters on the wall and emails go out and professors promote the things in class i never saw them that brings me to my final point if you go four years and never see any of the resources and opportunities there is some element of choice there some of it could be depression anxiety in which case counseling could be helpful which our college offers for free it s here it s free there isn t a months long wait but i can t make people go students will say well those things aren t required you re right they re not and you should see the fight and or straight up refusal to engage in those activities that of the class puts up when i have tried to make that type of thing required eg visit the career center so i stopped they re not required you re an adult make a choice and if a student s choice is to just exist they should know it is not likely to make them the strongest candidate for a job nor the most attractive romantic or platonic companion', 'i think where you start learning determines how much you understand from experience most schools teach bottom up rather than combining bottom up with top down it s tough for anyone to learn difficult new concepts without knowing why or attaching some level of usefulness beyond computation it helps me visualize when i know why i am learning a particular concept i made sure before i started my studies i spent almost a year watching on youtube every day minimum of an hour the big picture in all topics related to ai robotics then i followed people who work on these topics and break down their day to day how they got there what they studied etc after a year i was able to build a roadmap for myself for the technologies i needed to learn the subjects i needed to master and in what order after that i applied to graduate school i never stopped doing the daily youtube thing i would sometimes look at applications in mech civil eng that applied ai this really helped with the big picture for learning acronyms and stuff my past work experience taught me one thing underneath the hood it s the same math algorithms these topics have been around since forever the best way to learn for me was to read older books so i can understand the evolution and how these fields came to combine multiple disciplines and topics for example if you watch videos by people who have won the turing award a very prestigious award given by the association for computing machinery you will realize that computer science today is taught very differently from say unity boston dynamics tesla ai day on youtube research papers old ones and new ones especially those that do literature reviews and they explain a lot of the stuff in context i also read some textbooks that teach you the relevant math the theory and the application of cs topics these really helped a lot discrete mathematics and its applications th edition by kenneth h rosen is a perfect book especially if you come from traditional engineering fields that didn t use computers a lot numerical methods for engineers common title because the math is common to all fields these titles are great for engineers moving from civil mechanical into computing numerical methods in engineering with python by jaan kiusalaas mathematics for machine learning by marc peter deisenroth a aldo faisal and cheng soon ong these are great books in my opinion because they teach math real world applications from a cs point of view and they include a programming language like python matlab etc just search for them on google with pdf in the search and voila you re ready to go i have them all on my ipad so instead of wasting time on social media i can always read a chapter or two anytime before switching i worked over a year for a team of scientists who combined mechanical engineering solid mechanics to solve real world problems using ai and machine learning the teams were made up of mechanical engineers and computer science people they also built software to solve these problems e g digital twins which are virtual representations of actual environments this is a very important technology especially during the pandemic this is also very useful for civil construction one of the front runner companies where i live is a civil company they unity boston dynamics tesla ai day on youtube ad to virtual representation simulation environments actually coming from a different field of engineering is great because you have the context of problems that need to be solved most cs people are theoretical by nature and often need to work in an industry for a while before they can be able to practically apply their knowledge you can already start little projects related to your civil background that don t require much investment sensors e g are cheap free time i make sure that not a day goes by without me watching something for fun but touches on these topics following companies like matlab or nvidia unity boston dynamics tesla ai day on youtube and watching their free webinars seminars you will start to develop an intuition for the things you are studying and why how to apply them because the university can be so theoretical but hard for them to teach you everything', 'gt if you have time maybe you could clarify something for me i ve heard from a few people mostly on reddit that say that school is kind of a waste of time they say the schools can t keep up with the evolving tech which makes a lot of knowledge useless and they say you don t even really learn how to program i think these people are both right and wrong depending on various factors specifically the value of your degree can vary significantly from school to school some schools have subpar teachers and easy classes and so are worth very little and other schools do an excellent job of teaching a mixture of theoretical and practical skills without compromising on either obviously i have no idea where your school falls on this spectrum no matter how high quality your classes are i do think it would be a mistake to focus only on attending classes one of the main perks of going to a university or college is that it has a high range of extracurriculars to participate in ranging from joining a research lab many schools have undergrad research programs joining a club doing an internship ta ing a class these extracurricular opportunities are the main ways you make connections and develop a resume portfolio that ll help you distinguish yourself from other students if you don t take advantage of them and other events like career fairs you re not getting the full value out of your money and of course ideally you d join a school that does have a wide range of these opportunities it s true that the surface of programming can evolve rapidly new frameworks libraries programming languages come and go but tbh in the end there isn t that much substantive difference between them and the fundamentals of computer science and software engineering stay largely the same so if you have a good grasp of the fundamentals and have acquired the meta skill of being a good independent problem solver you shouldn t have a huge amount of difficulty picking up new things as necessary the people who tend to get overwhelmed by these surface level changes are usually the people who ve chosen to invest only in this surface level knowledge rather then the meta skill of rapid acquisition and mastery of knowledge of course there s no guarantee that your school will teach you these fundamentals force you to acquire these key meta skills see point i d argue that in a lot of cases it s actually industry that lags behind academia not the other way around for example paradigms like functional programming have been well studied in academia for decades but only really started seeing mainstream use during the past years or so or take machine learning most innovation here comes from researchers in universities or researchers who are a part of companies who have a dedicated research department the caveat here is that you typically don t learn about these cutting edge sort of things as an undergrad except maybe in your more advanced electives undergrad education and research can be pretty divorced from one another so if you want to learn about these sorts of topics you ll either have to finagle yourself into a grad level class which is doable in my experience teachers are willing to bend the rules for strong determined students try joining some research lab go to seminars and so forth in fairness most of these cutting edge things aren t really relevant to a large subset of jobs in industry most jobs are fairly straightforward if your goal is to just get a job you can probably get by if you just put in a moderate amount of effort into the standard classes this to be clear is a legitimate goal and strategy not everybody has an intrinsic interest in computer science or the financial stability and the free time to be able to invest a lot of time taking classes participating in extracurriculars and so forth and if this is your goal you re probably not as interested in the full span of what colleges and universities potentially have to offer and have more of a mismatch between what you pay and the value you get it s also true that there are some important skillsets that most universities and colleges struggle to teach things like being able to design a robust yet simple large scale system how to create and execute on a long term plan how to anticipate and mitigate unknowns how to properly roll out and maintain a production quality service to really teach these sorts of things i feel you have to make students live with the consequences of their earlier design decisions for a reasonably long span of time and it s hard to do that kind of thing in the span of just a single quarter or semester gt if you have any insight that you could provide on other classes that you think are particularly useful in addition to compiler design it would be really appreciated i really want to come out of this degree with the best knowledge possible i would recommend that you take classes that have a reputation at your school for being high quality the teachers are usually good and engaging the homework assignments tend to be challenging but interesting imo it doesn t matter what exactly that class is so long as it s at least mildly tangential to your interests having access to high quality course work can trump many other factors decide carefully if you want to go for breadth vs depth taking a bunch of classes can be fun and enlightening but you run the risk of spreading yourself too thin and never really acquiring mastery in anything never really being able to apply what you ve learned to do anything substantive conversely you also don t want to pigeonhole yourself into a single topic and narrow your field of vision take a discrete math how to write proofs class and a data structures algorithms class if they re not already mandatory if you plan on taking machine learning courses pay particular attention to your calculus statistics and linear algebra classes machine learning builds heavily on all three you take an operating systems course at some point or at least some sort of introductory here s how hardware and software interacts course machine learning and graphics both require you have an intuition on how to write efficient code and it s easier to do that if have some mechanical sympathy with your computer and operating system and again i think it s valuable to have some understanding of how your tools work from start to finish you take a course on how to implement a database assuming the course covers how to sanely and efficiently store and query data on disk and how to implement things like transactions both are useful and widely applicable concepts in particular it s useful to learn how to implement transactions because it forces you to learn how to deal with a world where your program can crash and abort at any moment this is a meta skill you ll also likely explore in your distributed systems classes actually so possibly you d only need to take one of the two unclear']

Name: 68_resume_projects_project_bullet
Representative_Docs: ['gt most if not all the projects are just passion projects i made these projects because i wanted to learn no one aside from me benefited from them there s got to be a reason behind the decisions you made why did you use a multinomial naive bayes model instead of just guessing numbers at random who benefits from automated maintenance logs and why why would someone use a movie review classifier etc show me well show the recruiter that you know how to solve real problems for real people tell me why your actions were necessary within the given context of the domain gt does it refer to the project or the tools i used to build the project should i write how i built my project or what i used it for can you give an example of what i should put here what you used utilized python how you used it to implement a machine learning sentiment analysis tool with numpy why you used it leveraging its native dimensional array tools and smaller memory footprint how someone benefited so that users can save money at the box office by validating critic reviews with a fast loading data visualization tool implemented css bootstrap to apply responsive functionality to existing html javascript components utilizing its declarative grid layout syntax and battle tested load performance so that users can perform custom searches on any device without waiting more than seconds for the app to load this approach makes for long bullet points but that s good aim for two full width lines each showcase your technical lineage in a way that impresses non technical recruiters gt i started coding very young i wouldn t dare say i have mastered all these languages but i have at least one project for each language except lisp on my portfolio and github should i still leave all the languages on the resume or do you suggest i trim the fat and keep just the ones i m really good at hmm adding years to your projects might help the danger is that you don t want to appear like a newbie developer who s just spilling a soup of terminology onto the page unfortunately there s not enough on the page here to prove that that s not true no offense if you ve been programming for a long time try to add a mix of old and new projects use the years to showcase your lineage over time don t forget to namedrop the language for each one and add multiple bullet points wherever you can that should help gt the recruiters who reached out to me so far are barely offering me anything over k k most of them claim my lack of any experience as the reason for the low offer and that it is standard for new grads with no internships it s certainly not standard well unless the people you re talking to are only from fully remote companies or pre seed startups they re making this pitch based on what s in your resume and on how you re presenting yourself if you ve indeed been programming since you were young then that means you re a much better programmer than most bootcamp grads and most bootcamp grads can make figures in new york city assuming that they were paying attention in class do you want to know what they have that you don t career coaching these soft skills make a huge difference be the accomplished unicorn engineer be the person who s been programming since you were a baby be opinionated and pivot each and every question to an anecdote about a project you worked on position yourself as someone who s already interviewing at the range that s the attitude you should take into each phone screen that s how you should approach writing your resume it s a tough thing to do though', 'you have some decent projects and based upon the rest of the contents of your resume you should focus on making this section the shining star of your resume i ll give you the same advice i got from the resume help desk at my university advice tailored towards new grads remove the objective priority from top to bottom should be technical skills certifications cs work experience if any cs intern exp if any degree declaration personal projects relevant non cs experience if you just graduated don t include any non cs work experience unless you keep it brief and are confident it relates to the job you apply for in other words remove your banquet server info flesh out your skills section what programming paradigms do you know oop dsa mvc what ide do you use version control git specializes libraries such as numpy streamline your project descriptions into bullet points embed links into the projects so people viewing the resume digitally can click to go right to the source github live website etc if your projects aren t live you need to fix that embed links into your social info github linkedin etc so that they can click to go to your socials make one resume blank file that you slightly customize for each job type if needed copy paste the job posting into chatgpt then provide it your copy paste resume and ask it to align your formatting and language to match the posting submit your resume as a word doc instead of a pdf as this increases your chances of getting by an automated keyword screener', 'you re not blacklisted i am reading this resume and i can say i have some serious problems with it i am speaking from the perspective of a fourth year undergraduate at uoftoronto so take what i say with a grain of salt though i apologize if i m coming across as a harsh critique but i hope you do find it helpful nonetheless the layout of this resume is terrible your technical skills are one of the most important areas in your resume and i honestly did not see them upon first glance of this resume jumbling them all in one isn t really a great way to do it what level of proficiency do you have with these languages you don t need to mention it you should have something such as and usually ordered by proficiency programming languages x y z frameworks x y z other x y z your education section is very bland for an intern the header also contains graduating which is really informal and off with the rest of the layout what sort of courses did you take you don t have to list your gpa but you should if it is over for example university of toronto honours bachelor of science sep apr computer science major gpa xx cgpa xx relevant courses operating systems algorithm design amp analysis neural networks web programming intro to ai database design just from the first of your resume a lot of work could be done here from an aesthetics perspective the bold font isn t consistent and all over the place and doesn t really flow well when reading i can barely focus reading it my main critique however is your claims on your resume i m not saying you didn t do what you said you did but i do have some serious doubts leading implementation of company s flagship really did you if you were an intern this would be your second internship it is a red flag for me that you are leading the implementation for a company s flagship product maybe you did do this or maybe you did not but i do have some doubts ultimately though i would say this line does not really reflect technical skill entirely analyzed customer and competitor data using linear regression with numpy in python helping secure largest b b client another very strange claim how did linear regression help you secure this client linear regression isn t a very strong statistical model and from what i gather here it seems you threw your data into a numpy matrix and passed it into a linear model statistical modeling can be quite theoretical and just building a linear model could actually comprise your entire internship how big was your data did you standardize your data what kind of predictors and dependent variables were you regressing on why is a linear model necessarily a great model for your problem what was your r a useful metric to list on resume when it comes to quantifying your results overall this claim seems to demonstrate a lack of understanding of statistics and data analysis in general which makes me think you just added this in for filler or because it sounded technical enough even if it did pass through some recruiters any statistician worth their salt will grill you on your linear model and ask you many detailed questions my suggestion would be to either improve and expand on what you did exactly or get rid of it outright but seeing as you did many other things during your internship i would still struggle believing this claim implemented an aes based encryption protocol via android ndk and c securing all critical xyz data no data is ever entirely secure i would suggest using the word improving security of xyz by lt some metric gt don t use words like via replace with using reduced execution time by hmm no you didn t your project section doesn t really flow well either you have technologies listed in the header don t do that have a dedicated line that says technologies python etc etc after your project description you should probably avoid hyperlinks as that would probably break parsers again i apologize if my critique is a little harsh on this i really think your layout could use some work i think some of your claims seem exaggerated part of my reasoning for this is because i had a similar resume to you last year where my accomplishments were certainly a lot more hyped up than i had intended for them to be and i ended up getting grilled at interviews or seeming overqualified after really focusing on the barebones of my resume did my interview rate skyrocket amp x b that being said i m no resume whizz nor do i browse this sub often so do take it with a grain of salt but i hope it helps nonetheless amp x b amp x b amp x b']

Name: 70_history_google docs_docs_version history
Representative_Docs: ['don t change your writing style to avoid getting flagged by an ai detector instead use a writing program like google docs that tracks edit history or save multiple versions using save as with different timestamps if using word if a professor accused you of ai show them the edit history or drafts to prove that you did your own work offer to meet with them to explain what you wrote and how you wrote it if they still don t believe you ask them to try to re create your essay using ai or run their publications or one of the course readings through an ai detector to show that false positives are real offer to write something on paper in front of them to prove it s the same writing style it sucks that honest students get falsely accused and professors should be more careful about putting too much faith in questionable ai detectors but if you truly did your own work you can defeat the accusation with evidence of your work process and knowledge', 'did you have any documentation supporting the fact that you didn t use ai for example your version history from google docs or microsoft word', 'for anyone in this situation who doesn t know this google docs keeps version history information go to file version history see version history as long as you write in google docs rather than writing in some other program and copy and pasting it should auto save a version every few minutes so you can show a professor or whoever that you wrote the essay out paragraph by paragraph rather than pasting it in from an ai generator incidentally always use google docs for group projects the version history also shows you who wrote what i ve seen google docs histories that showed students doing nothing on the whole project but adding their name at the top if you re asking the prof to give you more time easier grading whatever because a group member didn t help google docs is your best argument']

Representative_Docs: ['this is an automated service intended to preserve the original text of the post hi i am in a data science master s program and am taking an interesting class on machine learning this semester our classes meet for hours once a week in person and our professor is running the class in a very interesting manner all of my other classes have used those hours for lecture in the machine learning class the professor is posting all her lectures online a week before the class our job is to watch the lectures and follow along with her coding examples when we get to class we spend the whole hours in a lab working on an exercise very similar to her online lecture examples i actually like parts of this system because i like being able to pause the video lecture as i work out the examples myself in code during the in person lab we are supposed to work on our own and turn in the lab electronically before we leave it is sort of like an informal weekly quiz that lasts hours but the professor is available to answer questions and is quite helpful so it s kind of like a semi quiz in a very informal manner you can get full credit if you just make sure to watch her lectures the only issue i have is that our lab room is just not ideal for doing coding exercises everyone has to bring their own laptop and there are not enough power plugs for everyone who needs one in the room a lot of students have small screen laptops and they just aren t ideal for coding i am much faster coding when i connect my laptop to a large screen monitor at home and use a full size keyboard i ve talked to a lot of other students who feel the same way it is easier to have multiple windows open at once where you can refer to another piece of code as you work the labs are open book and open internet btw the only reason i can think the professor might want to have us in person is that she walk around the room and help out many students quickly however this can also be done via zoom through screen sharing i imagine she also wants to stop student collaboration but even during class student s could collaborate via text chat plus students are looking at each other s monitors and helping each other anyway and the prof doesn t seem to mind it just seems like it would be easier for everyone if we met remotely via zoom i like the professor and i think her lectures are well done and i am learning a lot and am interested in the field do any professor s here know why you would want to run a lab like this in person i am a bot and this action was performed automatically please none of those four are mandatory far more important would be all of the standard programming and computer science courses like algorithms data structures and linear algebra if you ve taken all of the core cs classes and linear algebra then the first two digital embedded systems and pdes could potentially be useful maybe time series analysis third because it s useful for some fields of ai and multivariate analysis last it s more if you want to go into math or mathematical modeling of science and engineering', 'you can write ai based applications without knowing any maths by using libraries the downside to this is that you won t really understand what s happening under the hood i took an ai module last year and i didn t really enjoy it but i think it s because i had to write boring applications like a basic email spam filter etc like you said ai is massive and there are a lot of areas you can study what most people think of when they think of ai is cool stuff like talking robots with emotions etc the reality is most ai they teach in school is just reactive ai not self aware ai like what you see in sci fi movies in my understanding no one really knows much about self aware ai where as we know a lot about reactive ai reactive machines can t perceive the past or form memories they just make predictions based on the present moment they can t use past experiences to inform their choices the reason you re seeing a lot of maths is because that s basically what makes ai systems tick if you wanna get into ai then you should look into the specific kinds of ai that interest you and study that learn the terminology along the way', 'i m very glad you provided specifics because i do think that changes the answer all ai and yes i will die on this hill is simply applied statistics even the most complex models are just compounded statistical decisions linguistic word processing bots just statistical predictions of word ordering neural networks weighted probability outcomes any and all forecasting model bayesian stats on steroids you can teach yourself python r ruby julia css c ect chances are you ll have to learn specific stuff in an advanced program anyway a lot of advanced ai programs make their own packages anyway these are all procedural tasks though to understand and contextualize the tasks though you will need a deep understanding of statistics writing useful algorithms is like making a sandwich good programming skills allow you to assemble a sandwich get out the bread put fillings in bread slice sandwich eat good statistical skills allow you to make a good sandwich choose the right type of bread for the sandwich decide home much of each filling choose an appropriate order taste the sandwich and revise your preferences i highly recommend you read weapons of math destruction to get some context on this knowing when to use which statistics is vital a lot of up and coming advanced tech grad programs are just as welcoming to someone with a background in statistics as software engineering computer science other readings automated inequality eubanks artificial unintelligence boussard the data detective harford future proof roose']

Name: 76_deep_deep learning_intelligence_artificial intelligence
Representative_Docs: ['is machine learning deep learning artificial intelligence do all of them imply the same thing', 'in general deep learning is a subset of machine learning and all of these are considered a part of artificial intelligence but each has a different definition i am still new to this field but according to my knowledge machine learning is the way the artificial intelligence learns something using a specific amount of data and then decide what to do out of the thing it learned deep learning is simply a part of machine learning', 'having completed my master s thesis on machine learning i can attest that machine learning is a process that attempts to automatically identify patterns within a given data set through exposure in other words machine learning involves creating computer systems that use advanced algorithms in order to produce a learning processes by exposing the computer system to information without the need for manual instructions on how to perceive the data simply machine learning gives computer systems access to data and lets them learn for themselves this allows machine learning systems to expand the capabilities of traditional statistical and probability models such as when systems need to handle data streams that are of large volume variety velocity and complexity e g subject to random variations artificial intelligence on the other hand is a broad term used to describe computer systems that are able to carry out tasks in a way that we would consider smart or rather intelligence hence the name expanding on this machine learning is often used to create more intelligence ai systems but machine learning systems are traditionally not considered artificial intelligence systems']

Name: 77_cs_ai_classes_major
Representative_Docs: ['hey r cscareerquestions my formal experience is limited to just apcs so far and i ve made a few programs and games on my own i m currently taking a gap year and regular decisions are already over for traditional colleges this year the idea of going to an online college seems really appealing to me especially western governors university and their competency based degrees however this degree is for computer science and afaik ai engineering heavily requires statistics and higher math i stopped at precalc plus other fields i have no formal education in the faq says certificates are useless for cs and i assume the lack of credibility issue carries over to statistics certificates furthermore i m not aware of any ai specific online degrees that allow me to skip paying for preemptively self taught content however i haven t done any further coursework on my own past apcs level yet so if it s possible to get an ai engineer job in the future after possibly a stepping stone one like software engineering or something i can vigorously self teach then enroll in wgu to attempt getting a degree as cheaply as possible after another gap year or two if that s possible otherwise i ll enroll asap then do whatever you all recommend for the stats math other aspects if there isn t a way to jump from a cs bachelor s to softdev to ai engineer i can bite the bullet and enroll in an ai engineering specific online master s degree programs like uni of san diego and arizona state uni after completing the wgu program and deal with the much higher tuition if you guys have any other suggestions or feedback even if unrelated to the title question they re very much welcome thanks in advance alex a', 'i m a fellow beaver who just graduated this summer so i had first hand experience on this topic i chose systems when i was at osu although the course load is quite rigorous i do not regret one bit systems requires some ece classes on digital logic and computer architectures i had friends who went with security and they seemed to like it a lot ai is a newly added specialization so i can t comment too much looking at the core classes it seems like the focus is more on statistics it is important to note that aside from the core classes listed in each specialization you are still required to take the core cs classes os software engineering algorithms etc on top of that you have to complete a certain amount of elective cs credits so you still have some flexibility in terms of the interesting classes you want to take for example i went for systems but i also took machine learning intro to graphics and numerical analysis as elective classes so let s say if you are taking systems but you are interested in security you can take intro to security cryptography and others as elective courses however you need to plan your classes in advance since some of the classes have pre requisite and some are offered in certain terms lastly only systems is abet accredited which means the systems curriculum meets the standard of abet in the end i suggest going for whatever you are interested in i recommend systems if you don t mind a little knowledge of hardware and digital logic in terms of job prospects i don t think there are many differences all of the specializations are still considered a cs degree since the core cs classes are the same and there are only minor differences in classes for each specialization on top of that most of them can be taken as cs electives anyway exception would be stats classes in ai spec not sure if that can fulfill cs electives credits anyway you will have to figure it out by the way take the advisor s words as a grain of salts not trying to be rude but sometimes they do not know what they are talking about the ai spec does not go deep into ai to get a job that uses cutting edge ai you need much more than the courses provided by the specialization and probably a higher degree anyway good luck to you and if you have more questions feel free to pm me', 'i already know that most employers don t really care if you have a bs or ba just if you can do the job or not my question is about the specific classes in each degree part of me would like to work on ai because i find it very interesting so maybe the bs would be worth it but maybe i don t need all the extra classes to land an ai position but then the other part of me says just get the ba and a less technical job that still pays well and do entrepreneurial stuff with that money like i ve always wanted i ll be done with my core classes after next semester so i have to make a decision about which road to pick soon here s a table comparing the required classes in each degree program that i haven t taken yet cs classes ba bs intro to oop intro to oop discrete math discrete math intro to data structures intro to data structures machine programming machine programming compilers and automata compilers and automata software dev software dev computing ethics computing ethics senior project senior project database management data structures and algorithms cs elective programming language structure cs elective architectural concepts cs elective operating systems cs elective cs elective cs elective non cs classes ba bs choice of calc technical calc or calc for business students calc calc choice of stats class with prereqs as low as college algebra choice of upper division stats class all with calc or prerequisites intro to linear algebra with calc prereq choice of upper division math class all with calc prereq or higher physical science with lab physical science with lab as you can see there are more credits to do in the bs as it requires more total credits and on top of that less of my transfer credit applies to it so i could graduate about a semester and a half earlier if i did the ba there s also a lot more math which makes me wary as i ve already failed calc in the past and withdrew from it once too many people say that calc is the most difficult math class next to diff eq which isn t encouraging either the only thing that really appeals to me about the bs is the linear algebra class which is used heavily in ai according to my reading is that something i could learn on my own or on the job or would i really need a fundamental understanding of calc first i d also like to point out with the ba electives i can choose any of the cs classes on the bs side another thing is it unlikely for a fresh grad to land an ai job right out of school aren t those jobs usually a more senior position for people with a lot of experience maybe i shouldn t even be worrying about that for a few years at which point the math classes i took in college wouldn t really matter all that much']
